content.title: ACC/AHA Special Report: Clinical Practice Guideline Implementation Strategies: A Summary of Systematic Reviews by the NHLBI Implementation Science Work Group

authors.name: Thomas A. Pearson, MD, MPH, PhD, FAHA
Harlan M. Krumholz, MD, ScM, FACC, FAHA
Sidney C. Smith, Jr, MD, FACC, FAHA

methodology_members.name: Julie C. Jacobson Vann, PhD, MS, RN

other_info: This document was approved by the American College of Cardiology Board of Trustees and the American Heart Association Science Advisory and Coordinating Committee in October 2016.
It was co-published in the journal Circulation.
This document is available online on the websites of the American College of Cardiology and the American Heart Association.

content.Background: In 2008, the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute convened an Implementation Science Work Group to assess evidence-based strategies for effectively implementing clinical practice guidelines. This was part of a larger effort to update existing clinical practice guidelines on cholesterol, blood pressure, and overweight/obesity.
content.Objectives: Review evidence from the published implementation science literature and identify effective or promising strategies to enhance the adoption and implementation of clinical practice guidelines.
content.Methods: The systematic review focused on 4 critical questions, each addressing the adoption and effectiveness of 4 intervention strategies: (1) reminders, (2) educational outreach visits, (3) audit and feedback, and (4) provider incentives. A scoping review of the Rx for Change database of systematic reviews was used to identify promising guideline implementation interventions aimed at providers.

Results.Audit Feedback and Educational Outreach: Generally effective in improving both process of care and clinical outcomes.
Results.Barriers: Included time constraints, resource limits, timing, clinician skepticism, and knowledge of guidelines.
Results.Facilitators: Included format, resources, end-user involvement, stakeholder involvement, leadership support, organizational culture, and electronic guidelines systems.

content.Conclusion: The strategies of audit and feedback and educational outreach visits were generally effective in improving both processes of care and clinical outcomes. Reminders and provider incentives showed mixed effectiveness or were generally ineffective.

content.Introduction: The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI) sponsored the development of clinical practice guidelines (CPGs) starting in the 1970s to promote application of research findings for cardiovascular, lung, and blood diseases. In 2008, expert panels were formed to update guidelines for high cholesterol, high blood pressure, and obesity using evidence reviews. By 2013, collaboration with the American College of Cardiology (ACC) and American Heart Association (AHA) was initiated to produce these guidelines.
content.Challenges: Uneven implementation of evidence-based CPGs is a challenge in improving public health. Patient adherence to recommendations is crucial for clinical outcomes.
content.Collaboration: The ACC/AHA Task Force on Clinical Practice Guidelines started updating these guidelines in 2014 as a response to emerging evidence.
content.Focus: The report focuses on key steps of provider adoption and adherence to clinical guidelines.

content.Overview: The guideline discusses implementation strategies for clinical practice guidelines (CPGs) influenced by education, visits, reminders, audits, feedback, and incentives. It details inclusion and exclusion criteria for reviews, focusing on studies affecting patient care directly. The search was limited to English-language resources.
content.Process: The ISWG maintained evidence separation in study reviews. Multiple researchers examined and abstracted data using standardized tools. The process included a rigorous validation for down-selecting reviews.
content.Data Analysis: Effectiveness was judged based on the proportion of positive outcomes, classifying interventions as generally effective, mixed effectiveness, or generally ineffective. For certain questions, insights were drawn from qualitative analyses.
content.Results: From 826 articles, 55 were selected, including 39 systematic reviews and 16 overviews of systematic reviews. They were rated for quality, with various scores determining their level of acceptance.

content.Critical Question 1: The effectiveness of selected intervention strategies was assessed, including educational outreach visits, reminders, audit and feedback, and provider incentives. Educational outreach visits showed general effectiveness in improving process-of-care outcomes, particularly in prescribing behaviors. Audit and feedback showed improved process-of-care and clinical outcomes. Reminders had mixed effectiveness for process-of-care outcomes but general ineffectiveness for clinical outcomes. Provider incentives showed mixed effectiveness, with most positive outcomes related to diabetes mellitus and asthma.
content.Critical Question 2: The cost considerations of implementing interventions were examined. Studies varied in how cost-effectiveness was measured, including savings per physician, cost avoidance, and hospitals' return on investment. Five good-quality and three fair-quality SRs provided information on intervention costs or cost reductions. Educational outreach visits were generally effective, while reminders had mixed effectiveness. Provider incentives reduced costs and were cost-effective.
content.Critical Question 3: Explored the contextual barriers that hinder or limit clinician adherence to guidelines.

4. DISCUSSION.4.1 Common Themes in the Evidence and Pr: The evidence shows a greater increase in CPG adherence in practices with low-baseline adherence. Multifaceted interventions tend to be more effective. Emphasis should be on helping implementers understand their current practices and how they may differ from forthcoming recommendations. A self-assessment toolkit could help in selecting strategies.
4. DISCUSSION.4.2 Report Limitations: Data were not retrieved from primary studies, limiting information on interventions. A qualitative synthesis was used, preventing size assessment of expected benefits. The report depended on judgments of SR authors and quality. Only 4 interventions were considered, and other interventions may have shown effectiveness. Some assessments included single and multifaceted interventions. Few evaluations provided potential influence information, and not many reported clinical effectiveness or cost outcomes.

content.3.4 Critical Question 4: Addresses how policy, regulatory, organizational, and financial factors influence the success of clinical-institutional interventions such as educational outreach, reminders, audits, feedback, and incentives. Multifaceted interventions may be more effective.
content.3.5 Summary: Summarizes the effectiveness of selected interventions. Multifaceted interventions are more effective. Many reviews don’t focus on CVD risk factors. Cost and effectiveness were not explicitly evaluated.

content.Translation Outlook 1: Audit and feedback along with educational outreach visits are generally effective for improving both process of care and clinical outcomes while provider incentives showed mixed effectiveness. Reminders showed mixed effectiveness for process of care and were generally ineffective for improving clinical outcomes.
content.Translation Outlook 2: Multifaceted interventions were more effective than a single intervention strategy.
content.Translation Outlook 3: Additional research is needed on intervention effectiveness with special emphasis on improving methods and study designs, increasing the use of pragmatic trials, leveraging electronic clinical data, and evaluating cost effectiveness of interventions.

content.General Information: The document is a special report on clinical practice guideline implementation strategies, published by ACC/AHA. The focus is on the effectiveness of different interventions, cost implications, and the role of systematic reviews in clinical practice. It reviews various studies on educational interventions, decision-support systems, audit and feedback mechanisms, and the impact of clinical practice on health outcomes. Specific areas of interest include adherence to asthma guidelines, venous thromboembolism prevention, and the reduction of cesarean sections.
Studies Reviewed: Educational interventions for arthritis clinical practice guidelines.
Audit and feedback effects on professional practice and healthcare outcomes.
Interventions to reduce primary care delay in cancer referrals.
Interventions increasing healthcare provider adherence to asthma guidelines.
Venous thromboembolism prevention in hospitalized patients.
Non-clinical interventions for reducing unnecessary cesarean sections.
Improving clinical practice via decision-support systems.

EPOC Definition of Intervention Strategi.Provider Reminders: Patient or encounter specific information designed to prompt a health professional to recall information.
EPOC Definition of Intervention Strategi.Academic Detailing: Use of a trained person who meets with providers to give information with the intent of changing provider's practice.
EPOC Definition of Intervention Strategi.Audit and Feedback: Summary of clinical performance with recommendations for clinical action.
EPOC Definition of Intervention Strategi.Pay for Performance: Financial reward or benefit for doing specific action.

content.description: The image presents a flowchart illustrating the selection of articles for inclusion in an ACC/AHA Special Report on CPG Implementation Strategies. It tracks the process from initial record identification through database searching and other means, to final selection for qualitative synthesis. Various reasons for exclusion, such as intervention/population mismatch, study type, language, and poor-quality rating, are noted. The effectiveness of interventions like educational outreach visits, audit and feedback, reminders, and provider incentives on process-of-care and clinical outcomes is summarized in a table.

tables.content: Summary of Systematic Reviews by Intervention, Effectiveness Rating, and Quality
Contextual Factors That Appear to Hinder the Success of the Intervention Strategies

factors: Timing and effectiveness—barrier to effectiveness if further away from point of decision making

tables.content: Contextual Factors That Appear to Facilitate the Success of the Intervention Strategies

factors: Involvement in planning, developing, or leading interventions
Provider incentives more widely implemented in the UK
Multidisciplinary teams, coordination of care, pace of change, blame-free culture

Suggested Actions.Title: Address Study Design Issues
Research Needs: Clear descriptions of study methods and the interventions
Explicit implementer and target of intervention
Standardized measures of outcomes and descriptions of practice settings

Suggested Actions.Title: Conduct New Research to Test the Effectiveness of Interventions
Research Needs: Effect on clinical outcomes, rather than intermediate outcomes
Effect of multicomponent interventions, including specific combinations of interventions
Effect of policy-level interventions, for example, reimbursement, accreditation, publicly reported quality metrics
Effect of interventions targeting varieties of settings, including baseline workflows, clinician types, types of diseases and conditions

Suggested Actions.Title: Leverage EHR Data and Tools
Research Needs: Aggregate data and/or interventions by key factors, for example, patient characteristics, clinician characteristics, clinic, healthcare delivery system

Suggested Actions.Title: Conduct Qualitative and Observational Research
Research Needs: Drivers of success in real-world implementations
Contextual issues not amenable to RCTs

content.context: This table details the healthcare-related relationships of authors with industry and other entities during the document development process (2012-2016) for the ACC/AHA Special Report on CPG Implementation Strategies. The document was reviewed and updated in conjunction with meetings and calls of the panel. It includes authors' employment, consultancy roles, honoraria, and research funding, emphasizing NIH and other institutional affiliations.
